questions.question: Why does the Meeting Prep Agent trigger 30 minutes before a call (instead of 2 hours or 5 minutes)?

options.text: To reduce API costs by batching requests
To give you time to read and internalize without forgetting details
Because calendar APIs only support 30-minute intervals
To avoid overwhelming you with too many prep docs

questions.explanation: 30 minutes is the sweet spot: early enough to review without rushing, recent enough that you retain the details during the call.

questions.question: What's the primary purpose of including fresh data (LinkedIn posts, news) in the prep doc?

options.text: To make the doc longer and more impressive
To surface recent trigger events for personalized talking points
To test if the data sources are working correctly
To replace the original research brief

questions.explanation: Fresh data (last 7-30 days) gives you timely hooks — e.g., 'I saw your post about attribution challenges' — that make your outreach feel current and relevant.

questions.question: Why does the agent generate 3 talking points instead of 10?

options.text: To save tokens and reduce cost
Because 3 is easier to remember and use in conversation
Because the LLM can't generate more than 3 reliably
To force you to prioritize manually

questions.explanation: 3-5 talking points is the optimal range: enough variety to adapt to the conversation, few enough to remember and deploy naturally.

questions.question: What framework does the agent use to order discovery questions?

options.text: BANT (Budget, Authority, Need, Timeline)
SPIN (Situation, Problem, Implication, Need-Payoff)
MEDDIC (Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Decision Process, Identify Pain, Champion)
AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action)

questions.explanation: SPIN is the discovery framework taught in Course 13. The agent orders questions from Situation (current state) → Problem (pain) → Implication (cost) → Need-Payoff (value).

questions.question: What happens if the agent can't find a matching case study for the prospect?

options.text: It generates a fake case study to fill the gap
It skips the Relevant Proof section entirely
It writes 'No matching case study available' and suggests creating one
It uses a generic testimonial instead

questions.explanation: The anti-hallucination instruction tells the agent to flag missing data rather than invent it. This prompts you to add a case study for that profile.

questions.question: The Meeting Prep Agent should join the call and take notes in real-time.
questions.explanation: False. This agent generates a prep doc before the call. The Post-Call Summary Agent (Lesson 7) handles transcription and note-taking.

options.text: True
False

questions.question: Including the email thread in the prompt helps prevent asking questions the prospect already answered.
questions.explanation: True. The email thread provides context on what's already been discussed, so you don't repeat questions or miss objections they've raised.

options.text: True
False

questions.question: At 5 meetings/week with Claude Sonnet, what's the approximate monthly cost for this agent?
questions.explanation: $5/month. At ~$0.02 per prep doc × 5 meetings/week × 4 weeks = $0.40-0.50/month. Negligible compared to the time saved (75-150 min/week).
